english: row place, valley, road; gu-yáṅs (-pa) (gu yangs (-pa)) spacious, roomy, wide, gu yáṅs-pa _dug (gu yangs pa 'dug) there is much room here.
Ts. plate, flat dish.
Amyris Agallocha, a costly incense, one kind is white, another black.
n. of a province in the south-western part of Tibet.
W. deaf (?).
Ssk., spiritual teacher, father-confessor.
Ld. colt or foal of an ass.
n. of a deity, resorted to by mothers for being blessed with children; acc. to Sch.: Siwa.
W. for gá-le (ga le) q.v.; gú-le-la (gu le la) id., slowly, softly, gently, without noise, *go gú-le-la cug* (*sgo gu le la cug*) shut the door gently! gu-yár (gu yar) Sch. apparently the same.
Wdk. garment, dress (?).
bent, bent downwards (?), of leaves Wdn.; gúg pa (gug pa) id.
W. to rub or scratch gently, to tickle.

I. Sch.: 'the broad-headed tiger of Central Asia, Charachula' (Mongol.); it is said to differ from stag (stag), and is not found in Tibet. — II. also dguṅ (dgung) (Cs. guṅ-ma (gung ma)) 1. the middle, guṅ-la (gung la) in the middle, e.g. the king in the middle (between his two wives); stód-kyi guṅ (-nas) tón (stod kyi gung (-nas) bton) taken out of the middle of the upper part Mil.; guṅ - du byéd - pa (gung du byed pa) Thgy. prob. to divide through the middle, to dissect (anatomically); guṅ sgríg-pa (gung sgrig pa) Sch. 'to unite'; with respect to time: dbyár-gyi guṅ-la (dbyar gyi gung la) W. in the middle of summer; nyín-guṅ (nyin gung), and mtsán-guṅ (mtshan gung) mid-day, mid-night Cs.; guṅ-gnyís (gung gnyis), the two middle times, mid-day and mid-night; nám-gyi guṅ-tún-la (nam gyi gung thun la) at the hour of mid-night. — 2. mid-day, guṅ báb-pa (gung bab pa) to take a noon-rest on a journey; guṅ-tsígs (gung tshigs) dinner Schr.; guṅ sáṅs-la gró-ba (gung sangs la gro ba) (W. *čá-če* (*ca ce*)) to take a walk about the middle of the day, at noon; perh. also generally: to take a walk; guṅ-lón (gung lon) Sch.: 'at noon', more prob.: afternoon. — 3. mid-night, guṅ-la (gung la) at mid-night Glr.; dguṅ-gčíg (dgung gcig) one night (?) Sch. — 4. (Chinese?) title of a magistrate in Lhasa, something like Privy Counsellor; v. dguṅ (dgung).
prob. = stag (stag) Ld.-Glr. Schl. fol. 13, 6.
n. of a monastery in Máṅ-yul (Mang yul) Mil.
the middle finger; *guṅ-dzúg* (*gung mdzug*) C. id.
C. carrot.
C. radish.

1. slope, declivity Cs. — 2. separation, solitude, seclusion Sch.; gúd-du bór-ba (gud du bor ba) to place obliquely Cs.; gúd-du gśégs-pa (gud du gshegs pa) Dzl. 220, 18 to separate, to disperse (?) Sch. — 3. C.: loss, damage = gun (gun), god (god). — 4. Ld.: heavy or thick of hearing, *gud-nág* (*gud nag*) quite deaf, deaf as a post. — 5. gúd-du jùg pa (gud du 'jug pa) v. gúd-pa (gud pa).
v. rgúd-pa (rgud pa).
(Cs. gún-pa (gun pa)) loss, damage, *ṅá-la gun póg* (*nga la gun phog*) W. I have suffered a loss (prop. damage has come over me).
Lh. expensive, dear.
a bottle-shaped or cylindrical basket to put fruit in, Ld. (perh. akin to rkón-pa (rkon pa)).
v. _gúm-pa ('gum pa).

resp. bźugs-gúr (bzhugs gur), gzims-gúr (gzims gur) Cs., also dbu-gúr (dbu gur) C., tent, gos-gúr (gos gur) Cs. a tent of silk, pyiṅ-gúr (phying gur) of felt, sbra (sbra) and re-gúr (re gur) of coarse yak's hair felt, ras-gúr (ras gur) of cotton cloth; rgyal-gúr (rgyal gur) Cs. 'a king's pavilion', dmag-gúr (dmag gur) a military tent. — gur-mcóg (gur mchog) a magnificent tent, or gur-rgyál (gur rgyal), is used by Chr. Prot. for the tabernacle. — gur-tág (gur thag) the tent-ropes, *gur-bér* (*gur ber*) W., or gur-śiṅ (gur shing) Cs. the tent-poles. — gur-tóg (gur thog) Cs.: 'the upper covering or outer fly of a tent'. — gur-gźól (gur gzhol) Cs.: 'the walls of a tent'. — gur-klád (gur klad) passage for the smoke out of a tent, gur-
